patient_profile.therapeutic_framing: Dual proteinopathy means MORE therapeutic targets, not fewer. Cross-seeding between alpha-synuclein and tau means therapies targeting either protein could benefit both pathologies.

trial_enrollment_guide.status_note: ClinicalTrials.gov shows 'Not Yet Recruiting' (last updated Nov 2025) but CurePSP announced Q1 2026 enrollment. The registry often lags — call the enrollment center for real-time status.
trial_enrollment_guide.why_this_trial: AADvac1 is a tau vaccine — it directly targets the protein causing the PSP features (backward falls, eye movement problems). The trial has 75% chance of active drug (3:1 randomization), covers transportation and hotel costs, and after 12 months ALL participants (including placebo) get the option to receive the real drug.
trial_enrollment_guide.key_insight: IMPORTANT: The trial accepts 'possible' PSP Richardson's — not just 'probable.' This is a LOWER diagnostic bar and may fit your dad's current presentation. His neurologist is already a movement specialist who performed the biopsy and MRI — they may already have or can quickly provide the diagnosis needed.

enrollment_center.name: ATRI Recruitment & Retention Unit (USC)
enrollment_center.role: Central enrollment for all ~50 trial sites nationwide. They can tell you real-time status, nearest site, and start eligibility screening.

curepsp_hopeline.name: CurePSP Hope Line
curepsp_hopeline.role: Nonprofit partner for trial recruitment. Can answer questions, connect you with a trial site, and provide support resources.

trial_enrollment_guide.what_to_say: My father has been diagnosed with Parkinson's disease but also has features consistent with PSP Richardson's syndrome — backward falls and eye movement abnormalities. His neurologist is a movement disorder specialist who has already performed a skin biopsy (positive for alpha-synuclein) and brain MRI. We're interested in the PSP Platform Trial evaluating AADvac1 (NCT07173803). Can you help us determine eligibility and find the nearest trial site?

steps.title: Call or email the enrollment center
steps.description: Call 213-821-0569 or email psp-participate@usc.edu. Use the script above. They handle enrollment for all ~50 trial sites and can tell you real-time recruitment status and the nearest site to your dad.

steps.title: Contact the neurologist about PSP diagnosis
steps.description: Your dad's neurologist is already a movement specialist with biopsy and MRI results. Ask: 'Based on [dad's] backward falls and eye movement issues, could he meet criteria for possible or probable PSP Richardson's syndrome under the 2017 MDS criteria? We're interested in a clinical trial (NCT07173803) that requires this diagnosis.' Show them this link: https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT07173803 — The existing MRI may already show relevant findings (midbrain atrophy / 'hummingbird sign').

steps.title: Sign up for CurePSP trial updates
steps.description: Register at https://www.psp.org/ptp to get trial status updates by email. Also call the Hope Line (800-457-4777) — they exist specifically to help families navigate this process.

steps.title: Gather documentation for screening visit
steps.description: When a site schedules a screening visit, bring: (1) Current medication list — especially carbidopa-levodopa dosing, (2) Skin biopsy results — the positive phosphorylated alpha-synuclein report, (3) Neurologist records — symptom history, onset dates, progression notes, (4) Brain MRI results already performed, (5) Timeline of when falls and eye movement issues started — must be less than 5 years, (6) The care partner who will attend all visits (a family member must commit to accompanying him).

steps.title: Also ask about the AZP2006 arm and prasinezumab
steps.description: Don't put all eggs in one basket. The same trial also tests AZP2006 (different mechanism, same sites). Also ask about prasinezumab Phase 3 for the alpha-synuclein side: https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04777331 — contact Roche/Genentech trial sites. Your dad's movement disorder specialist may know of additional trials at their center.

eligibility.requirement: Clinical diagnosis of possible or probable PSP Richardson's syndrome (2017 MDS criteria)
eligibility.dad_status: Needs formal PSP evaluation — but his symptoms (backward falls, eye movement abnormalities) fit. 'Possible' PSP is sufficient. Neurologist may already have enough to diagnose.

eligibility.requirement: Progressive symptoms for fewer than 5 years
eligibility.dad_status: Need to confirm timeline of when falls and eye movement issues started
Needs to be ambulatory at time of screening

eligibility.requirement: Care partner available for all visits
eligibility.dad_status: Family member must commit to attending every trial visit

trial_benefits: 75% chance of receiving active drug (3:1 randomization, not 50/50)
After 12 months, ALL participants (including placebo) get option for active drug
Transportation costs covered by the trial
Hotel/accommodation costs covered by the trial
Up to 50 trial sites nationwide — enrollment center will find the nearest one

action_items.title: PSP Platform Trial — Call Enrollment Center NOW
action_items.description: Call 213-821-0569 or email psp-participate@usc.edu to start the enrollment process for the AADvac1 tau vaccine trial (NCT07173803). See the Trial Enrollment Guide at the top of this page for the complete step-by-step instructions, what to say, and eligibility checklist.
action_items.contact: ATRI Enrollment Center: 213-821-0569 / psp-participate@usc.edu | CurePSP Hope Line: 800-457-4777

action_items.title: Discuss Prasinezumab Phase 3 Trial Enrollment
action_items.description: Roche's prasinezumab (anti-alpha-synuclein antibody) Phase 3 PADOVA trial is enrolling 900 patients at sites across the US. This targets the CONFIRMED alpha-synuclein pathology. Ask the neurologist about eligibility and nearby trial sites. ClinicalTrials.gov: NCT04777331.
action_items.contact: Roche/Genentech clinical trials; neurologist referral to trial site
action_items.deadline: Currently enrolling — discuss at next neurology visit

action_items.title: Order Comprehensive Genetic Testing (WGS/WES + PD Panel)
action_items.description: Genetic testing is the KEY that unlocks personalized medicine. It reveals druggable variants (GBA → ambroxol/venglustat, LRRK2 → kinase inhibitors), HLA type for vaccine compatibility prediction, MAPT variants affecting tau biology, and SNCA variants affecting alpha-synuclein expression. Without this data, all therapy selection is generic. With it, we can computationally predict which vaccines and antibodies will work best for THIS patient specifically.
action_items.contact: Step 1: Ask neurologist for referral to genetic counselor. Step 2: Order Invitae Parkinson's Disease Panel (invitae.com/en/find-a-provider) — often $0 with insurance for PD patients. Step 3: If budget allows, request Whole Genome Sequencing (WGS) through a research program like GP2 (gp2.org) or commercial provider (Nebula Genomics, Dante Labs). Step 4: Request HLA typing if not included.
action_items.deadline: Order at next neurology visit — results take 2-4 weeks

action_items.title: Request Tau PET Imaging + CSF Biomarker Panel
action_items.description: These diagnostic tests would clarify the extent of tau pathology and quantify alpha-synuclein seeding activity. Tau PET (flortaucipir) visualizes tau distribution. CSF alpha-synuclein seed amplification assay (SAA) quantifies seeding. CSF phospho-tau panel (p-tau 181, 217, 231) quantifies tau burden. These results inform trial eligibility and treatment strategy.
action_items.contact: Current neurologist; may need referral to academic movement disorder center

action_items.title: Discuss Lixisenatide (GLP-1 Agonist) with Neurologist
action_items.description: Lixisenatide showed reduced motor disability progression in NEJM 2024 Phase 2 trial (LixiPark). While not yet approved for PD, it's available as a diabetes medication and the neuroprotective evidence is strong (Tier 1). Some neurologists are prescribing off-label. Discuss risk/benefit with the care team.

action_items.title: Monitor ACI-7104.056 (VacSYn) Phase 2 Expansion
action_items.description: AC Immune's alpha-synuclein vaccine showed exceptional results: >20x antibody levels, 100% seroconversion, no UPDRS III progression at 74 weeks. Stage 2 expansion to 150 patients is expected. No action needed now, but this could become enrollable within 6-12 months. Keep monitoring.

action_items.title: Explore Dendritic Cell Vaccine Feasibility
action_items.description: Preclinical research shows dendritic cell vaccines can be dual-loaded with alpha-synuclein AND tau antigens — potentially addressing both pathologies simultaneously. This is still early-stage (Tier 3) but the dual proteinopathy makes this approach uniquely relevant. Ask the neurologist or an academic center about any open trials or compassionate use.

tracked_therapies.name: ACI-7104.056 (VacSYn)
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Peptide vaccine using SupraAntigen platform — mimics oligomerized alpha-synuclein conformation to generate antibodies that clear pathological aggregates
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Interim data: >20x anti-α-syn antibody levels vs baseline, 100% seroconversion rate, no UPDRS III motor score progression at 74 weeks. Stage 2 expansion (150 patients) expected.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Directly targets confirmed α-syn pathology. Strong immunogenicity data suggests robust antibody response. If approved, could slow or halt synuclein-driven degeneration. Cross-seeding benefit possible (reducing α-syn may reduce tau templating).

tracked_therapies.name: UB-312
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Peptide vaccine targeting C-terminal region of alpha-synuclein using UBITh platform technology
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Phase 1b study in PD patients nearing completion. Safety and immunogenicity data expected in 2025.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Another α-syn vaccine approach — different epitope than VacSYn. If VacSYn fails, UB-312 may succeed due to targeting different region of the protein.

tracked_therapies.name: PD01A / PD03A (AFFITOPE)
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Short peptide mimicking α-syn epitope (AFFITOPE technology) — generates antibodies without autoimmune risk of full-protein vaccines
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Demonstrated immunogenicity and acceptable safety profile in Phase 1. Further development status under review by AFFiRiS.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Third α-syn vaccine approach. Lower autoimmune risk due to short peptide design. Earlier stage but diversifies the vaccine pipeline.

tracked_therapies.name: AADvac1
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Tau peptide vaccine targeting mis-disordered tau protein — selected for PSP Platform Trial based on Phase 1 safety and immunogenicity data
tracked_therapies.status: PSP Platform Trial Phase 2a — ENROLLING Q1 2026
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Selected as one of two regimens for the PSP Platform Trial. Enrolling at UCSF, UCSD, and Mass General. This is the first dedicated PSP immunotherapy trial with a tau vaccine.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: HIGHEST RELEVANCE — directly targets suspected tau pathology (PSP features). The PSP Platform Trial is specifically designed for patients with PSP-Richardson's phenotype. Enrollment is NOW.

tracked_therapies.name: AZP2006
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Restores lysosomal homeostasis and modulates progranulin — addresses tau clearance mechanism rather than directly binding tau
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Selected as the second regimen for the PSP Platform Trial alongside AADvac1. Novel mechanism targeting tau clearance rather than tau itself.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Relevant for PSP features. Different mechanism than AADvac1 (clearance vs. immunotherapy) — if one fails, the other may work through a different pathway.

tracked_therapies.name: Anti-pT181 Mucosal Vaccine
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Nasal mucosal vaccine targeting phospho-tau181 epitope — non-invasive delivery, targets specific phosphorylation site implicated in tau seeding
tracked_therapies.latest_update: University of New Mexico developing mucosal (nasal) vaccine against phospho-tau181. Preclinical data encouraging. Enrollment expected early 2026.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Targets tau phosphorylation specifically implicated in PSP. Non-invasive nasal delivery is attractive for patients with motor symptoms. Earlier stage but novel approach.

tracked_therapies.name: Prasinezumab
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Monoclonal antibody targeting aggregated alpha-synuclein — binds and promotes clearance of extracellular α-syn aggregates
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Roche's Phase 3 PADOVA trial is the largest α-syn immunotherapy trial in history. 900 patients across global sites. Phase 2b showed 28% slowing of motor progression in rapid progressors. Phase 3 expanded to broader PD population.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Directly targets confirmed α-syn pathology. Most advanced α-syn antibody in clinical development. Phase 3 enrollment is ACTIVE — should discuss trial participation with neurologist.

tracked_therapies.name: Bepranemab
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Anti-tau monoclonal antibody targeting central region of tau protein — binds both 3R and 4R tau isoforms
tracked_therapies.status: Open-label extension in PSP — completing Nov 2026
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Open-label extension study in PSP patients ongoing, expected completion November 2026. Safety and preliminary efficacy data expected.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Targets tau in PSP specifically. One of few monoclonal antibodies being tested in PSP (not just AD). Completion in Nov 2026 means results could inform treatment decisions.

tracked_therapies.name: Brain-Shuttled α-Syn Antibody
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Engineered antibody with transferrin receptor binding domain — crosses blood-brain barrier at 10-40x higher brain concentrations than standard antibodies
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Published in npj Parkinson's Disease 2025. Brain-shuttle technology achieves dramatically higher CNS penetration. Could solve the BBB problem that limits current antibody therapies.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: If current antibodies (prasinezumab) fail due to poor brain penetration, brain-shuttled versions could succeed. Technology platform could be applied to both α-syn and tau antibodies.

tracked_therapies.name: Lixisenatide
tracked_therapies.mechanism: GLP-1 receptor agonist — activates neuronal survival pathways, reduces neuroinflammation, promotes neurotrophic factor release. Originally a diabetes medication.
tracked_therapies.latest_update: NEJM 2024 (LixiPark trial): Lixisenatide reduced motor disability progression vs placebo over 12 months. First GLP-1 agonist to show disease-modifying signal in PD. Further Phase 3 trials in planning.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Neuroprotective regardless of protein pathology — could benefit both α-syn and tau-driven degeneration. Already approved for diabetes (available off-label). Discuss with neurologist.

tracked_therapies.name: Exenatide
tracked_therapies.mechanism: GLP-1 receptor agonist — same class as lixisenatide but different pharmacokinetic profile
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Lancet 2025: Phase 3 trial of exenatide in PD was NEGATIVE — did not slow progression. However, different GLP-1RAs may differ in efficacy. Lixisenatide's positive result suggests the class isn't dead.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Negative result for this specific GLP-1 agonist, but the class remains promising (lixisenatide was positive). Highlights that not all GLP-1RAs are equivalent for neurodegeneration.

tracked_therapies.name: BIIB101 (Anti-SNCA ASO)
tracked_therapies.mechanism: Antisense oligonucleotide targeting SNCA mRNA — reduces production of alpha-synuclein at the genetic level rather than clearing existing aggregates
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Ionis/Biogen antisense oligonucleotide reducing SNCA expression. Phase 1 study in MSA (another synucleinopathy). If successful, could expand to PD and dual proteinopathies.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Upstream approach — stops α-syn production rather than clearing aggregates. Could be combined with immunotherapy (reduce production + clear existing). Currently in MSA but PD expansion possible.

tracked_therapies.name: Focused Ultrasound BBB Opening
tracked_therapies.mechanism: MRI-guided focused ultrasound transiently opens the blood-brain barrier, allowing antibodies and other therapeutics to reach brain tissue at much higher concentrations
tracked_therapies.status: Phase 1 safe in PD dementia — combination trials next
tracked_therapies.latest_update: Phase 1 demonstrated safety of focused ultrasound BBB opening in PD dementia patients. The next frontier is combining FUS with immunotherapy — open the BBB, then deliver antibodies directly to affected brain regions.
tracked_therapies.relevance_to_patient: Could dramatically improve efficacy of any antibody therapy (prasinezumab, bepranemab, brain-shuttled Abs). Particularly relevant for dual proteinopathy — could deliver both α-syn and tau antibodies in same session.

research_updates.title: Personalized Genomic Medicine Roadmap — From Generic to Patient-Specific Therapy Selection
research_updates.summary: Added a 3-phase roadmap for personalized medicine. Phase 1: obtain comprehensive genetic data (Invitae PD Panel + WGS via GP2 program + HLA typing). Phase 2: computational analysis including variant interpretation, drug-gene interaction screening, HLA-epitope vaccine compatibility prediction (using NetMHCpan — a technique borrowed from cancer immunotherapy, novel for neurodegeneration), and AlphaFold3 structural modeling of any patient-specific variants. Phase 3: re-rank all 13 tracked therapies using a composite score combining genetic relevance, HLA compatibility, drug interactions, and evidence tier — producing a truly personalized treatment ranking.
research_updates.implications_for_patient: The immediate action is to order genetic testing — this is the single step that unlocks everything. The Invitae PD Panel is often free with a PD diagnosis. The GP2 program offers free whole genome sequencing. Once we have the data, we can build computational pipelines to predict which of the 13 tracked therapies are most likely to work for THIS patient specifically, based on their unique genetic profile and immune system.

research_updates.title: Initial Research Compilation — Dual Proteinopathy Treatment Landscape
research_updates.summary: Comprehensive analysis of the treatment landscape for dual alpha-synuclein + tau proteinopathy. Identified 13 tracked therapies across 6 categories: 3 alpha-synuclein vaccines (VacSYn, UB-312, AFFITOPE), 3 tau-targeting approaches (AADvac1, AZP2006, anti-pT181), 3 monoclonal antibodies (prasinezumab, bepranemab, brain-shuttled Ab), 2 GLP-1 neuroprotectants (lixisenatide, exenatide), 1 gene therapy (BIIB101), and 1 delivery technology (focused ultrasound). The key insight: cross-seeding between α-syn and tau means therapies targeting either protein could benefit both pathologies. The dual proteinopathy is an OPPORTUNITY — more therapeutic targets, not fewer.
research_updates.implications_for_patient: The treatment landscape for dual proteinopathy is the richest it has ever been. Two trial enrollment windows are open NOW (AADvac1 for tau, prasinezumab for α-syn). GLP-1 neuroprotection (lixisenatide) is available today. The next 12-24 months will see VacSYn expansion, bepranemab results, and focused ultrasound combinations. The strategic priority is: (1) enroll in available trials, (2) pursue biomarker clarification, (3) discuss neuroprotection with the care team.

genomic_roadmap.title: Personalized Genomic Medicine Roadmap
genomic_roadmap.description: Step-by-step plan to move from generic treatment landscape to PERSONALIZED therapy selection using the patient's own genetic data, computational biology, and AI-driven analysis.

phases.urgency: Do this NOW — everything else depends on it

steps.title: Request genetic counseling referral
steps.description: Ask the neurologist for a referral to a genetic counselor who specializes in neurodegeneration. The counselor will explain what tests are available, what the results might mean, and help navigate insurance coverage.
steps.how: At the next neurology appointment, say: 'I'd like a referral to a genetic counselor to discuss comprehensive genetic testing. We want to identify any variants that could affect treatment selection or qualify for gene-targeted clinical trials.'

steps.title: Order Invitae PD Panel (clinical-grade, often free)
steps.description: Invitae offers a Parkinson's Disease genetic panel covering GBA, LRRK2, SNCA, PARK2/Parkin, PINK1, DJ-1, VPS35, and other PD-associated genes. For patients with a PD diagnosis, copay is often $0 through their patient assistance program.
steps.how: The genetic counselor or neurologist orders this. If ordering directly: visit invitae.com, search 'Parkinson's Disease Panel', and use their provider portal. Results arrive in 2-3 weeks as a clinical report with variant interpretation.

steps.title: Enroll in GP2 for free Whole Genome Sequencing
steps.description: The Global Parkinson's Genetics Program (GP2) offers FREE whole genome sequencing to PD patients at participating sites worldwide. GP2 is funded by the Aligning Science Across Parkinson's (ASAP) initiative. WGS provides complete genetic data — far more than a panel — enabling all downstream computational analyses.
steps.how: Visit gp2.org and click 'Participate'. Find a participating site near the patient. Contact the site coordinator to enroll. They will collect a blood or saliva sample and return WGS results. This is a research program, so results may take longer (2-6 months) but provide the most complete data.

steps.title: Alternative: Commercial WGS if GP2 unavailable
steps.description: If GP2 doesn't have a nearby site, commercial whole genome sequencing is available. Nebula Genomics (~$299 for 30x WGS) or Dante Labs (~$199) provide raw data files (VCF/BAM) that we can analyze computationally. These are research-grade, not clinical-grade — results should be confirmed by clinical testing before making treatment decisions.
steps.how: Order online from nebula.org or dantelabs.com. They ship a saliva collection kit. Mail it back. Raw data (VCF file) arrives in 4-8 weeks. Download the VCF file — this is the input for all our computational pipelines.

steps.title: Request HLA typing
steps.description: HLA (Human Leukocyte Antigen) typing reveals which immune system molecules the patient has. This determines which peptide fragments from alpha-synuclein and tau the patient's immune system can present to T cells — critical for predicting vaccine response. HLA typing is standard in transplant medicine and cancer immunotherapy.
steps.how: HLA type is embedded in WGS/WES data (we can extract it computationally using tools like OptiType or HLA-HD). If only doing a panel test, ask the genetic counselor to add HLA Class I (A, B, C) and Class II (DRB1, DQB1, DPB1) typing. Clinical labs (Quest, LabCorp) offer standalone HLA typing — often covered by insurance if requested by a physician.

phases.urgency: Ready to build as soon as genetic data arrives

steps.title: Variant interpretation pipeline
steps.description: Upload the VCF file from genetic testing. Filter for neurodegeneration-relevant genes (GBA, LRRK2, MAPT, SNCA, GRN, C9orf72, TMEM106B, and ~50 others). Annotate each variant with: ClinVar pathogenicity classification, gnomAD population frequency, CADD score (deleteriousness), protein impact prediction (SIFT, PolyPhen-2).
steps.how: We will build an automated pipeline on the dashboard. Input: VCF file. Output: prioritized list of clinically relevant variants with drug interaction annotations. Tools: bcftools, ANNOVAR or VEP (Ensembl Variant Effect Predictor), ClinVar API, gnomAD API.

steps.title: Drug-gene interaction screening
steps.description: Cross-reference the patient's genetic variants against the Drug-Gene Interaction Database (DGIdb), PharmGKB, and ClinicalTrials.gov to identify: (1) FDA-approved drugs that target the patient's specific variant pathways (repurposing candidates), (2) clinical trials specifically recruiting patients with these variants, (3) pharmacogenomic interactions that affect drug metabolism (e.g., CYP2D6 variants affecting levodopa metabolism).
steps.how: Automated pipeline: query DGIdb API (dgidb.org/api) and PharmGKB API with each significant variant. Cross-reference with ClinicalTrials.gov 'genetic' eligibility criteria. Output: ranked list of drugs and trials matched to the patient's genome.

steps.title: HLA-epitope vaccine compatibility prediction
steps.description: THIS IS THE NOVEL ANALYSIS. Using the patient's HLA type, predict which peptide epitopes from alpha-synuclein and tau protein will bind most strongly to their specific HLA molecules. Then cross-reference against the exact epitopes used in each trial vaccine (VacSYn, UB-312, AADvac1, anti-pT181). Result: a personalized ranking of which vaccines are most likely to generate a strong immune response in THIS patient.
steps.how: Tools: NetMHCpan 4.1 (HLA-peptide binding prediction, state of the art), MHCflurry 2.0 (deep learning alternative). Input: patient HLA alleles + peptide sequences from each vaccine. Output: predicted binding affinity (IC50) for each vaccine epitope × patient HLA combination. We display this as a vaccine compatibility matrix on the dashboard.

steps.title: AlphaFold3 structural variant modeling
steps.description: If the patient has any missense variants in SNCA, MAPT, or GBA, use AlphaFold3 to predict how these variants affect protein structure. Map variant positions onto the known alpha-synuclein and tau fibril structures (from cryo-EM). Visualize using interactive 3D protein viewer (Mol*/3Dmol.js) embedded in the dashboard.
steps.how: Use AlphaFold3 server (alphafoldserver.com) or ColabFold for structure prediction. Compare wild-type vs. variant structures. Overlay with known drug binding sites and vaccine epitope locations. This is educational AND could reveal if a variant affects a drug target site.

steps.title: Re-rank all 13 tracked therapies by patient-specific relevance
steps.description: Using genetic data + HLA compatibility + drug-gene interactions, re-score every tracked therapy. A therapy that targets a pathway affected by the patient's specific variants ranks higher. A vaccine whose epitopes bind strongly to the patient's HLA ranks higher. A drug with pharmacogenomic interactions gets flagged.
steps.how: Composite scoring algorithm combining: (1) genetic variant relevance score, (2) HLA-epitope binding affinity, (3) drug-gene interaction strength, (4) evidence tier, (5) enrollment availability. Display as a personalized therapy ranking on the dashboard — replacing the generic evidence-tier sort with a patient-specific sort.

steps.title: Generate personalized clinical trial eligibility report
steps.description: Many clinical trials have genetic inclusion/exclusion criteria (e.g., 'GBA carriers only' or 'LRRK2-negative required'). With genetic data, we can automatically filter ClinicalTrials.gov to show ONLY trials the patient is genetically eligible for.
steps.how: Parse ClinicalTrials.gov eligibility criteria for genetic requirements. Match against patient genotype. Filter the tracked therapies table to highlight trials with confirmed genetic eligibility. Flag any trials the patient is EXCLUDED from.

steps.title: Discuss personalized report with neurologist
steps.description: Bring the personalized treatment ranking, vaccine compatibility prediction, and drug-gene interaction report to the neurologist. These computational findings should INFORM the clinical conversation, not replace it. The neurologist may identify clinical factors that modify the computational ranking.
steps.how: Print or share the dashboard page. Walk through: (1) genetic variants found, (2) which trials the patient is genetically eligible for, (3) which vaccines are predicted to work best with their immune system, (4) any drug repurposing candidates. Ask: 'Given these results, which trials should we prioritize?'

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: Visualize tau protein distribution in the brain. Can confirm PSP pattern (subcortical tau in basal ganglia, brainstem, cerebellum) vs Alzheimer's pattern (cortical tau). Critical for trial eligibility and treatment planning.
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Major academic medical centers with PET imaging capability. May require referral from neurologist.

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: Quantify alpha-synuclein seeding activity in cerebrospinal fluid. Complements the positive skin biopsy by measuring how actively α-syn is misfolding and spreading. Higher seeding activity may indicate faster progression or better response to anti-α-syn therapy.
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Research labs and some academic centers. Lumbar puncture required.

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: Quantify tau phosphorylation burden. Different phospho-tau species reflect different aspects of tau pathology. p-tau217 is the most specific for tau aggregation. Results help quantify the 'tau side' of the dual proteinopathy.
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Clinical labs (Quest, LabCorp offer some; research panels at academic centers more comprehensive). Lumbar puncture required.

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: THE KEY TO PERSONALIZED MEDICINE. Screen for genetic variants that unlock gene-targeted therapies AND enable computational analysis. GBA mutations → ambroxol, venglustat trials. LRRK2 mutations → kinase inhibitor trials (LRRK2i). MAPT variants → tau biology insights, PSP trial stratification. SNCA variants → α-syn expression levels, ASO eligibility. Full genome enables drug-gene interaction screening and HLA-based vaccine compatibility prediction.
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Option A (Clinical Panel): Invitae PD Panel — often $0 copay with PD diagnosis, results in 2-3 weeks. Order via neurologist or genetic counselor. Option B (Research WGS): GP2 Global Parkinson's Genetics Program (gp2.org) — free WGS for PD patients at participating sites. Option C (Commercial WGS): Nebula Genomics (~$299) or Dante Labs (~$199) for 30x whole genome — provides raw data for computational analysis.

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: Determines which immune system 'locks' the patient has — critical for predicting which vaccine peptide epitopes (the 'keys') will generate the strongest immune response. Enables computational prediction of vaccine compatibility: which trial vaccines (VacSYn, AADvac1, UB-312, anti-pT181) use epitopes that this patient's immune system can best recognize and respond to. Standard in cancer immunotherapy, novel application for neurodegeneration.
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Usually included in WGS/WES data. Can also be ordered separately via clinical labs (Quest, LabCorp) or specialty HLA labs. Often covered by insurance if medically indicated. Some genetic testing panels include HLA as part of the report.

diagnostic_recommendations.purpose: Quantify dopamine transporter density in the striatum. Confirms and quantifies dopaminergic neurodegeneration. May be required for eligibility in some clinical trials (prasinezumab Phase 3 requires DaT-SPECT confirmation).
diagnostic_recommendations.availability: Nuclear medicine departments at most hospitals. Outpatient procedure.
